data.title: JPIE: Checking-In On One Of Our Holdings
data.description:
Thesis

JPMorgan Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JPIE) is an active fixed income ETF that we hold in our portfolio. The name is a core holding for us, and we last covered the fund in late 2024. In today's article we are going to re-visit the name, its performance, changes in the portfolio composition and strategy as well as our view on the fund given the current macro context.

A good start to the year

JPIE has posted a healthy start to the year, with a 2.85% total return:

We are comparing the fund to several other active ETFs with similar duration, namely the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC), the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) and the iShares MBS ETF (MBB). JPIE has outperformed so far the utilized cohort, albeit by a small margin.

If we look back at the ETF's historic performance, we will notice the name only lost money in its inception year when rates moved up aggressively:

  • 2022: -6.13%
  • 2023: +7.07%
  • 2024: +6.32%
  • 2025: +2.85% (YTD so far)

More importantly, the fund had a very shallow drawdown during the April 2025 market sell-off, with the ETF down only -1% from a total return perspective (chart above). If we look at pure price performance for a 'clean' drawdown profile, we get the following:

On a price basis, the fund's drawdown was slightly higher at -1.6% (from +0.8% to -0.8%), but in-line with its very conservative risk metrics of 2.37% annualized volatility and 2.1% standard deviation, which an investor can find on the 'Risk' tab in the Seeking Alpha platform.

Portfolio managers are keeping the duration profile low

The portfolio duration is now down to 2.83 years as per the fund's analytics:

Duration represents a portfolio's sensitivity to interest rates, and the lower the duration profile, the lower the impact of rising rates on the fund. We like this profile because it is unclear what will happen to rates, especially long rates. Front rates (2- and 3-year) are very well correlated to Fed Funds, so they will eventually move lower. However, intermediate and long rates might go a bit higher if inflation spikes again. We articulated this view extensively in a different research article, namely the 'GOVI: Back To The Future' piece.

The JPIE ETF is active, meaning that the portfolio is changing as per the manager's direction and views, but it is a rates-driven one:

Via its high-quality composition, the fund takes very little credit risk. We can see that below investment grade paper represents only 23% of the holdings, with mostly BB names accounting for that slice. The vast majority of assets are AAA, and we can see from the sectoral parsing that these are MBS bonds:

Agency MBS securities represent 36% of the portfolio, followed by CMBS at 19% and HY Corporates at 14%. The fund looks for low duration assets that provide for a spread to treasuries, and takes little credit risk. We clearly saw this composition kick-in during the April 2025 turmoil, with the drawdown very shallow.

Fund forward

With a 30-day SEC yield of 6.3%, the ETF represents a good vehicle to realize carry (i.e., interest income). The fund's build supports that view, with an overweight positioning in high quality, low duration assets that offer a higher spread than risk-free assets. The name has very shallow drawdowns, and has rates as its main risk factor. We are penciling in a total return in excess of 6% for 2025, in-line with prior figures and the fund composition. An aggressive Fed that would unexpectedly cut several times in 2025 would provide for an additional +3% if 3-year rates move lower by 100 bps.

While some might characterize JPIE as 'boring', the name represents a core component of a balanced portfolio, offering very high returns for the units of risk it takes. Investors should understand they will not make 10% plus per annum here with JPIE, but should expect returns in the 6% to 8% bracket, dependent on rates movements and portfolio manager's trading acumen.

The market has a very favorable view on JPIE, with a steady increase in AUM since inception:

Other analytics for the fund are as follows:

  • AUM: $3.8 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.39%
  • 30-day SEC yield: 6.3%
  • Portfolio yield: 6.46%
  • Duration: 2.8 years

Conclusion

JPIE is an active fixed income exchange-traded fund. The vehicle comes from J.P. Morgan, and has seen its AUM soar since inception. We like the fund's low duration of 2.8 years and its active approach to portfolio construction. Furthermore, we are of the opinion that the front of the yield curve is attractive currently, with spiking deficits impacting intermediate and long rates rather than front yields. JPIE is not an exciting ETF, but a reliable one. The name had a very shallow drawdown during the April 2025 market meltdown, and is to be perceived as a carry vehicle. We are penciling in a total return in excess of 6% for 2025, and still consider the name a 'Buy'.

data.title: Dollar Trends Lower While Loonie Gets Dragged - North American Mid-Week Market Update
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The Dollar index is consolidating below the 100.00 level for the second consecutive week, as Tuesday’s rally in the USD was not strong enough to hold. The DXY is trading at 98.80 and hasn’t crossed above 100 this week.

Between renewed menaces and the actual delay in implementing tariffs, US President Trump is making sure that the ongoing trend of USD selling doesn’t stall.

The announcement in the middle of last week from the US Federal Court boosted the USD initially, though the appeal of the court decision just added to more uncertainty.

Overall mixed US Data hasn't helped to add demand for the Greenback.

Manufacturing PMI and JOLTS beat while ADP Employment and Services PMI, entering into contraction, missed - everybody looking to price in cuts would focus more on the negatives, especially with 70% of the US Economy being service oriented, and the JOLTS data being a month-old (as the data released is from the past month).

Equity Indices Snapshot

US and Canadian Indices have had quite a positive week with a generally positive sentiment from markets, also propelling Industrial Metals, Oil and traditionally more risk-on currencies like the AUD and NZD.

I would take a look at the S&P 500 for a guide to this week's appetite for buyers as we approach the 6,000 Milestone - Momentum will find a few hurdles as traders brace for the upcoming Non-Farm Payroll report on Friday, 6th.

Breaking the level before the data gets released will show that markets are looking at other themes like the de-escalation of trade tensions (i.e. Taco Trump).

Still, expect a lot of volatility as all eyes are on the upcoming NFP report.

Bank of Canada June Meeting

There was the Bank of Canada rate decision this morning, the BoC held rates at 2.75% and are still waiting for more insights on the impact of US Tariff policies.

There has been a bounce in Canadian consumption and hiring, but the Central Bank would like to see more - in the meantime, there is still a cut about halfway priced in for the July meeting, which won't materialize if the data holds.

The May headline inflation report came in at 1.7%, below the 2% target though the BoC's favourite Core CPI is still at 2.5%.

There isn't any particular citation from Macklem or Rogers to note.

US Dollar Mid-Week Performance vs. Majors

USD vs other Majors, June 4, 2025 - Source: TradingView

The Greenback is onto another losing week, down against all majors.

There has been some particular strength from Asian-Pacific currencies, boosted from the pushback of US import tariffs on many Chinese goods all the way to August 31.

Canadian Dollar Mid-Week Performance vs. Majors

CAD vs other Majors, June 4, 2025 - Source: TradingView

The Loonie got somewhat dragged down by the USD and is losing the most against the strong APAC currencies. The Canadian Dollar is still broadly unchanged against European Majors.

The absence of a cut this morning gives the CAD some fundamental strength, though markets will be looking at Canadian employment data coming at the same time as the NFP before moving the currency forward.

Intraday Technical Levels for the USD/CAD

USD/CAD 1h Chart, June 4, 2025. Source: TradingView

USD/CAD broke below the past week's lows and has started to form a downward channel with decent selling momentum.

Currently trading around 1.3680, there isn't much to retain the USD/CAD from stabilizing below the 1.37 level and moving down further towards the 1.36 psychological level.

The hourly RSI is back to neutral from oversold, and EMA 20 and 50 are acting as Resistance.

There are rumours of a trade deal coming up between the US and Canada, potentially next week.

I would focus on the current trend before the data, though do be cautious on the double Employment report on Friday.

US and Canada Economic Calendar for the Rest of the Week

As discussed before, all eyes are on the double employment report coming in on Friday at 8:30 A.M E.T.

US Non-Farm Payrolls are expected at 130K and Canada is expecting a drop of 15K.

The CAD tends to move on the Ivey PMI Data that gets released tomorrow at 10:00 A.M., therefore keep this one in check. It's expected at 48.3, already in contraction territory with last month's report at 47.9.

For the rest, a few Fed Speakers with only Kugler being a voter for 2025 and the weekly Jobless Claims report coming up tomorrow.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PVH: Valuation Does Not Outweigh Another Disappointment (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of PVH Corp (NYSE:PVH) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about a third of their value as the company has struggled with weak consumer sentiment, pressures in China, and uneven performance in the US. Investors received more bad news Wednesday night when PVH slashed guidance to a surprising degree alongside Q1 results. I last covered shares in December rating the stock a “buy” as I felt that its cheap valuation more than factored in guidance I found concerning. In hindsight, I should not have left valuation lead me to overlook fundamentals with shares dropping 24% since my recommendation. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit PVH.

In the company’s fiscal first quarter, PVH earned $2.30, which beat estimates by a nickel as revenue grew by 1.5% to $1.98 billion. Both metrics beat guidance of flat-to-down sales and $2.10-$2.25 of EPS. PVH took a noncash impairment of $480 million, largely due to high interest rates, which was excluded from non-GAAP results. While results beat guidance, they were not particularly strong in an absolute sense.

After all, adjusted EBIT fell from $195 million to $160 million. While revenue was up by $32 million, gross profit fell by $37 million to $1.16 billion. As a result, gross margins compressed by 280bps to 58.6%, given a mix shift towards wholesale and a need to do more promotional activity. Partially offsetting this, PVH has shown solid expense discipline. SG&A was up just 0.6% to $1.024 billion. Adjusting for restructuring costs, SG&A was own over 0.5%

Looking at its major brands, Tommy Hilfiger grew by 3% to $1.05 billion. Meanwhile, Calvin Klein was flat at $886 million. This has continued a theme of PVH struggling to generate as much growth from CK, and its brand rebuild remains a work in progress. Across channels, wholesale was up 7% while DTC fell by 3%. Its own stores were down by 5% while digital sales rose by 3%. This shift towards wholesale is a negative for margins, and ultimately, it appears to be timing-related, rather than a sign of enduring strength.

The more notable variance in performance came from its regional outcomes because PVH is a genuinely global brand. In the Americas, revenue was up 7%, due to strength in wholesale, which was primarily a pull-forward in shipment timing. This region is just over 30% of sales. Direct-to-consumer sales fell by mid single-digits, a better indicator of underlying performance in my view. Even with sales growth, profits fell by nearly 10% to $61 million, given the lower margin nature of wholesale activity.

In Europe, the Middle East, and Africa, revenue grew by 5% thanks to strength across wholesale and DTC channels. This is the biggest unit, representing nearly half of sales. Despite the revenue growth, pre-tax income here was flat at $149 million, speaking to the magnitude of margin pressure the business has faced, even in its strongest performing region.

While PVH did well in the Western world, is Asia performance was quite poor. In Asia, revenue was down 13%, including 2% of currency headwinds. With this decline, the region accounted for less than 20% of sales. There was a 3% timing headwind from the earlier Lunar New Year, but even absent this, sales were down 8% on a constant currency basis. Given deleveraging from lower sales, profits fell by over 22% to $79 million.

This is very weak, and China was particularly soft. PVH, of course, was placed on China’s “unreliable entity” list in response to initial Trump Administration tariffs, which likely weighed on activity. As noted on its last earnings call, this was a fairly unprecedented action. PVH is working with local authorities, and it remains committed to the Chinese market. With souring US-China relations, I have become increasingly concerned that US brands face structural brand equity destruction in this critical market that will be difficult to turn around.

While not a great quarter by any means, this was not particularly out of step with expectations. However, the outlook was disappointing. Alongside results, management reaffirmed revenue guidance of flat to slightly higher. However, it now expects just an 8.5% operating margin from at least 10% previously. Accordingly, EPS is expected to be $10.75-$11.00 from $12.40-$12.75. It sees $1.05 of unmitigated tariff impact, or $65 million.

Because PVH gets the majority of its revenue from overseas, it actually has less tariff exposure than most retailers as they only impact domestic sales. Ultimately, there should be some mitigation actions, by shifting production and taking some price, so the true tariff impact should be less than $0.50, or 5% of earnings, a fairly measured amount. However, it took its forecast down about $1.70, or more than 3x the likely tariff impact. This means the business is performing worse, even without tariffs.

PVH is guiding to significant underlying margin pressure that cannot simply be explained away by tariffs. Management blamed an uncertain consumer and much more promotional activity. It also appears to have misgauged demand. It now carries $1.6 billion of inventory, up 19% from last year, even with sales only modestly higher. PVH built up inventory for a sales boost that never happened. That means PVH likely will to cut prices and sacrifice margin to move product, especially in Asia where sales are weak. This is how it will maintain revenue guidance but see significantly lower operating margins.

I expect the worst of the margin pain to occur in Q2. Management expects just $1.85-$2.00 in Q2 EPS, including $0.20 of tariff headwinds. Earnings will be down over 33% from last year. From Q2 levels, there should be some sequential improvement. Still with relatively modest economic growth, I hesitate to assume much recovery. PVH has simply been caught flat-footed in this more tepid environment, and it will take several months to rectify the issue, and longer to restore credibility.

Management credibility is further impaired by the fact that this mess was reported only shortly after doing a $500 million accelerated buyback. In total, PVH did $561 million of buybacks in Q1, and it will not allocate more cash to buybacks this year. As such, buyback demand will not support shares much further. When all shares are delivered, there should be about a 14% share count reduction. PVH carries $191 million of cash on hand and just over $2.3 billion in debt. We should see debt come down by ~$200 million by year end. PVH pays just a token 0.2% dividend.

Frankly, I am cautious on its ability to mitigate any of the tariff impact, the future of wholesale deliveries in the US following a strong Q1, and capacity for China to rebound. As such, I am assuming run-rate EPS of no more than $10 over the next 12 months. Fundamentally, it is hard to like the stock, especially as it will take management several quarters to rebuild trust. Excess inventory will also take at least 2-3 quarters to work through.

At 7.5x, valuation seems too cheap to ignore, but Macy’s (M) is below 7x, and The Gap (GAP) is below 10x. In other words, PVH is not really notably cheap in a retail sector filled with underperformers and single-digit multiples. Looking past fundamental missteps and to valuation has been a mistake thus far, and this is not a business likely to turn around in the next two quarters.

Given that, I am inclined to think shares are likely to languish in the $70’s, and I believe PVH is more likely to be value trap than value opportunity. As such, I am moving shares to a “hold” With its valuation and solid balance sheet, I can’t endorse selling shares here, but I just see no rush to jump into the stock and buy into this dip.

data.title: Alphabet: The Moat Wall Street Overlooked
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Alphabet’s (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) AI overhaul is quietly redefining the economics of search, and Wall Street hasn’t priced it in. While the stock is down since the last coverage, Q1 FY25 results reveal a powerful inflection: AI-driven ad formats like Overviews and multimodal tools such as Circle to Search are boosting conversion and unlocking new monetization surfaces.

With over 1.5 billion users engaging with AI-enhanced search, Google is not just defending its moat, it’s expanding it. Yes, $75 billion in CapEx brings short-term margin strain, but it also lays the foundation for Alphabet’s dominance in AI infrastructure, ad tech, and cloud.

Ad Revenue Growth and Higher Conversion Leads to Scalable Monetization Expansion

Alphabet’s moves to integrate AI into core search and ad products have preserved its market share against competitors and opened new topline sources. For instance, in Q1-FY25, Search and other ad revenues grew 10% YoY to $50.7 billion despite macro uncertainties and increasing head-to-head competition with AI-first search alternatives like Perplexity and ChatGPT.

Alphabet’s topline growth was based on advancements in AI Overviews. This feature now serves 1.5 billion users/month across 140 countries and 15 languages. The expansion of AI Overviews has boosted Google’s capability to answer complex queries, leading to higher commercial search volume.

Alphabet’s AI-driven search maintains monetization rates relative to traditional search. This is vital because it disproves the bearish tone that AI-generated answers can reduce ad impressions. Instead, Google has sharply embedded ads within AI Overviews. How? AI Overviews ensures that sponsored links remain visible even as search pattern evolves.

Additionally, the company’s experimental AI Mode allows for more advanced reasoning and multimodal responses. The mod has seen users submitting queries that are twice as long as traditional searches. So, this is pointing out that AI is not replacing search but it is boosting search depth and utility. This factor creates a new market for advertisers to target more specific user intents

Moreover, there is a rapid adoption of multimodal search tools like Circle to Search and Google Lens. For instance, Circle to Search is now available on over 250 million devices and had a 40% QoQ increase in usage. Whereas Lens recorded an additional 5 billion monthly visual searches since October FY24. These tools are shaping users' interaction with search. They are moving beyond text to incorporate images, voice, and real-world context.

For advertisers, this shift presents new spots for engagement in e-commerce. Here, visual search can directly link users to products. Google’s Demand Gen AI-powered ad product has already delivered strong performance with a 26% YoY increase in conversions per dollar spent for advertisers. In short, these multimodal search tools may become core ads topline growth drivers amid the rising digital ad spend.

Case studies (like Royal Canin’s campaign) hit a 2.7x higher conversion rate and a 70% lower cost per acquisition that highlight the sharpness of AI-optimized ads. The strength of Alphabet’s ad business is reinforced by its scale in search. With over 5 trillion annual searches, Google remains the dominant gateway for online discovery, while competitors like OpenAI and Anthropic are exploring AI-native search experiences, none have yet matched Google’s scale and ability to seamlessly integrate ads into AI responses without degrading user experience.

Furthermore, Google’s investments in LLMs (like Gemini 2.5 Pro) lead industry benchmarks in reasoning and coding tasks. This ensures that its AI capabilities remain best-in-class. The 200% growth in active users for AI Studio and Gemini API since the beginning of FY25 points to the developer and enterprise adoption of Google’s AI tools that will embed its ecosystem more into business workflows. Looking ahead, Alphabet’s AI search and ad business, coupled with its vast data advantages and infrastructure, creates a moat that may expand the total addressable market (TAM) for ads, particularly in sectors like travel, retail, and financial services. A complex moat that competitors cannot easily breach.

In short, Alphabet’s first-mover advantage in AI-powered search monetization maintains its lead in digital ads in upcoming quarters. With these elements Alphabet’s earnings remain stable and stay on the course as observed in the analyst EPS estimates. Considering the forward EPS annual growth, GOOG is highly undervalued relative to the long-term average (forward P/E of 20x - 5 year average) based on the forward PE ratio of 17x. Also, the street sentiment is highly positive with 37% upside at the high-end of the price target for the next 12 months.

The $75 Billion Bet: Alphabet’s AI Spending Spree Tests Wall Street’s Patience

Alphabet’s aggressive CapEx introduced near-term financial pressures that can hit the bottom line. In Q1-FY25, the company had $17.2 billion in CapEx with a significant increase YoY. The majority of CapEx is allocated to AI infra, data centers, and next-generation TPUs. For FY25, Alphabet may spend $75 billion on CapEx. That is up from ~$55 billion in 2024, pointing to a focus on scaling AI and cloud capabilities. However, these investments are already contributing to rising depreciation expenses that grew 31% YoY in Q1 and may accelerate further throughout FY25.

The primary concern is that higher depreciation can cap margin expansion, even if revenue grows. For instance, Google Cloud had its operating margin improve to 17.8% in Q1 (up from 9.4% YoY), but this progress could slow as depreciation from new data centers and servers impacts bottomline. Additionally, the cloud business is currently operating in a tight supply-demand environment, meaning topline growth (28% YoY in Q1) may fluctuate depending on the timing of capacity deployment. Now larger-scale infra rollouts are expected in late FY25 can lead to uneven quarterly performance in the near term.

However, this weakness also serves as a necessary trade-off for forward growth. For instance, Alphabet’s investments in custom AI chips like Ironwood (its 7th-generation TPU) may reduce long-term compute costs while improving performance. In short, Ironwood delivers a 10x improvement in compute power against previous generations while being twice as power-efficient.

This will lower the cost of running AI models at scale. Similarly, Alphabet’s partnerships with Nvidia (to secure next-gen Blackwell and Rubin GPUs) ensure its lead in AI training and inference. These investments are needed to keep pace with Microsoft and Amazon in the cloud race and to future-proof Google’s AI infrastructure as demand for AI services grows.

Beyond cloud and AI, Alphabet’s CapEx is also funding growth in Other Bets. In particular, Waymo is scaling rapidly. Waymo now handles over 250,000 paid passenger trips per week (up 5x YoY) and is expanding into new markets like Atlanta and Washington DC.

Although autonomous vehicles are still a loss-making segment, the deal with Uber and plans for personal ownership models suggest long-term monetization potential. Similarly, investments in YouTube’s subscription business (now at 270 million paid subs) and Google’s hardware ecosystem (Like Pixel and Nest) diversify topline beyond advertising.

In short, Alphabet’s high CapEx and depreciation are near-term headwinds, but they are vital for the long term for maintaining its lead in AI, cloud, and next-generation tech. One should weigh the short-term margin pressure against the long-term benefits of infrastructure scalability and market lead.

If Alphabet succeeds in leveraging these investments (through higher cloud adoption, more efficient AI services, and breakthroughs in autonomous driving) the payoff can justify today’s spending. However, if ROI weakens, the company’s heavy investment cycle can face scrutiny and this will hit hard on the stock valuations.

Takeaway

Alphabet is quietly building the infrastructure to dominate AI search, cloud, and ad tech for the next decade. While $75 billion in CapEx pressures margins today, its AI-first products are already boosting conversions, deepening moats, and expanding monetization. Wall Street sees the cost, investors should focus on the compounding payoff.

data.title: Nutanix: Growth Beyond The Hype
data.description:
As Nutanix (NASDAQ:NTNX) trades around all-time highs, it becomes a question beyond value to be an investor now. The thesis should answer potential for growth from here on, compared to investments made post pandemic. The stock has had a relatively quiet year in 2024 compared to the Nasdaq 100. Whether that is a lull before the next leg up or further consolidation is what I will try to answer in this analysis.

Industry and Macro Environment

Nutanix's hybrid multicloud solution to simplify enterprise IT is a secular tailwind that could yet last in terms of total addressable market and growth opportunities. However, competition is also fierce (VMware Broadcom (AVGO), Dell Technologies (DELL), Microsoft (MST) and Cisco (CSCO), to name a few established names) and there's no true moat that cannot be penetrated. Differentiation exists in its offerings being platform and hardware agnostic to a large extent. However, there's no wide moat, particularly seen in comparison with larger players in the generic cloud space with proprietary cloud plays like AWS and Azure. Competition with players with deeper pockets and bigger ecosystems means Nutanix will have to grow organically over time and rely on customer stickiness and cross selling to grow.

The macro threat of reduced IT spending is less of a concern. Given Nutanix's offerings supporting what I believe to be the foundational or core IT layer in enterprises (computing, storage and networking), it should be able to withstand IT discretionary spending pauses. Hybrid solutions also reduce total cost of ownership while providing regulatory and reputational must-have security requirements against data sovereignty. As I see it, the biggest opportunity for Nutanix is among mid-sized enterprises, where the need for one-stop simplicity and flexibility is the highest. Smaller enterprises will mostly keep relying on on-premise infrastructure.

The opportunity for Nutanix opens up even in larger enterprises who were using VMWare and are facing issues around new license structures, price hikes, uncertain support and reliability going ahead. Nutanix's transparent pricing, flexibility (no lock-in) and lower complexity of offerings could see a huge interest as customers switch over from competition. However, given the transition hurdles and stickiness resultant thereof, the move over to Nutanix could take time and even see some drop offs if VMWare keeps allaying customer pain points in the upcoming months.

Key strategic partnerships with Cisco, Dell Technologies, and Pure Storage (PSTG) strengthen its platform capabilities and market reach, as highlighted in the recent earnings call.

Valuation with Digressing Stories

For valuation comparisons, I will still use erstwhile numbers of delisted VMWare and Hashicorp as they resemble the software only solutions business of Nutanix. Diverging for a bit while on VMWare and Hashicorp, two interesting angles are worth mentioning. Nutanix is almost a leading company now, focusing solely on hybrid cloud software solutions. While the clear focus helps build niche expertise and perception for Nutanix, VMWare and Hashicop (now with IBM (IBM)) will gain from cross selling opportunities among a larger client base of the new entities they're part of (maybe after initial hiccups). Plus the resource-rich advantages of a larger ecosystem.

The other interesting story is how Pure Storage is shaping up as both a partner as well as a competitor for Nutanix. With complementary offerings where they do not overlap, alliances to meet complex customer demand are not uncommon in the hybrid cloud business. It somewhat emphasized that the space is not a zero sum game where one large player takes over the market. There's room for decent growth for all players, prioritizing and solving for complementary customer needs. Partnering while still vying for the same wallets.

Coming back to valuations, NTNX currently trades at a significantly higher EV to forward revenue multiple of 7.9x compared to a peer like Pure Storage at 4.5x. This is understandable as Nutanix is seen as a subscription-based revenue generator relying on higher gross margins pure play in software. Pure Storage is transitioning into software and still has hardware legs to its business.

Nutanix also had a transformation into a more pure software play in its earlier days (from hardware offerings also as a focus point), which kept its valuation grounded in the past few years. It never saw the SaaS hype peaks that HashiCorp saw on its open source expectations. IBM's acquisition at lower valuations led to a reality check, and HashiCorp ended its listing close to Nutanix's valuations. Despite growth, as seen in HashiCorp, Nutanix was never valued above VMWare given the latter's deeper entrenched markets compared to Nutanix's early software foray days. All in all, Nutanix shows a history of no pumped up valuations. Recent highs relative to its own history are also not outrageously high and could be building in for higher growth short-term expectations of grabbing market share from VMWare post acquisition. Not the optimal valuations, but risk levels appear low in the overall context of the SaaS universe.

Financial Indicators Look Good

Q3 2025 results show a strong growth of 22% on its topline with 18% ARR growth. Revenue growth has been healthy for quite a few quarters now, even as NTNX scales. The recurring revenue from a subscription-based model is a big tick. Guidance for the last quarter of the FY is also positive, a revision upwards. The VMware effect of customers shifting into Nutanix is not hugely visible in top-line growth numbers. As pointed out earlier, it's a long lead process and may not translate fully as well. In fact, with time, VMWare and HashiCorp will potentially pressurize new market share for Nutanix given its larger ecosystem. The story is neither hugely threatening, nor materially bullish for NTNX, in a cloud space where dynamics are usually stable and changes don't happen one-sided toward a single provider or overnight.

Gross margins are looking extremely good, underlining the margin growth at scale story for Nutanix. Guidance on margins and cash flow also remains positive. Full year operating margins are expected at 20.5% with free cash flow of $715m at the midpoints.

NTNX Post Results Still a Hold

Despite a broad positive outlook, NTNX first corrected and then ended mostly flat in post market trade after results. This despite delivering strong results and raising full-year guidance. I believe NTNX shares have not broken out from current levels due to a mix of valuation concerns and investor expectations. The stock had already rallied nearly 29% year-to-date, prompting profit taking. I see no concerns around the valuations for the positives Nutanix offers.

Growth numbers look good, guidance is conservatively strong and the growth factors are supportive for future expansion as well. Cloud infrastructure solutions are relatively well shielded from any spending cut impact in an unhelpful macro scenario as well. It's more of a strategic necessity than a discretionary expense. So, while growth may decelerate marginally in the short term, the segment is unlikely to face significant long-term contraction, especially as businesses continue to prioritize their digital transformation efforts.

Overall, NTNX is a Buy. I'm comfortable with the stable dynamics of the cloud market as a support against the lack of any clear moat for NTNX. Valuations could have been better, but not a deal breaker. Buy with a long-term mindset on the secular tailwinds alone, if not the Nutanix story, with the ability to absorb short-term consolidation or corrections. The risk reward appears favorably aligned for fresh investments.

data.title: Plains All American Pipeline: A Great Option For Its Dividends
data.description:
Plains All American (NASDAQ:PAA) is an American company dedicated entirely to the midstream business of oil and gas transportation. The company maintains a presence with its pipelines in both Canada and the United States, although it concentrates the bulk of its business in the latter. As we will see later, the company's infrastructure is located primarily throughout the Permian Basin, from Alberta (Canada) to Texas (US).

As a pure-play MLP (midstream operator), Plains demonstrates a fairly stable cash flow thanks to the medium- to long-term contracts it maintains with oil and gas production companies. At the same time, this stability and predictability in the company's revenue is combined with a solid and well-developed infrastructure that primarily requires maintenance investments. All of this allows the company to reward shareholders with excellent dividend payments, as I will demonstrate throughout this report.

Thesis

As I mentioned earlier, Plains is a pure-play MLP, something I find truly attractive in a context of high volatility and sharp declines in international oil prices. Plains is one of the midstream companies with the highest market capitalization in the American market, with gas pipelines that crisscross North America from Alberta, Canada, to Texas in the United States. Analyzing the map below, you can see that the company maintains a presence throughout the Permian Basin, positioning it as a strategic infrastructure provider for the region's major oil and gas producers.

Now, from my perspective, midstream companies are attractive at a time when international fossil fuel prices are extremely volatile. While the drop in oil prices may have a short-term impact on the revenues of the basin's main producers, this scenario doesn't apply to companies like Plains. Infrastructure demand will continue, and there are a number of medium- and long-term contracts that offer a stability advantage in this context of increased price fluctuations.

In this sense, the question arises as to whether demand for fossil fuels will remain at current levels in the short and medium term or whether it will decline in the future. In the latter case, we could assume that the company's revenues could be affected in the short term. One thing we see is that the world's leading energy agencies maintain that demand for oil and gas will remain robust and even slightly increase in the future. As can be seen in the figure below, all of them, with the exception of the IEA, maintain that demand for fossil fuels will remain at current or higher levels until 2050.

Now, I think it's important to analyze the scenario from the supply side, because that's what will allow us to understand the situation of a company located in the middle of the production process, as is the case with Plains. When we look at the role of the United States as a producer, we see that it's one of the main producers. If we take into account that the Permian Basin is one of the main basins in the country, we understand the strategic role of this type of company. From a geographical perspective, the basin is located in the center of the country, which places it at a certain distance from export ports. In this context, fuel transportation infrastructure is essential.

Now, let's move on to what I consider to be one of the most important points when it comes to seeing Plains' potential compared to other companies in the fuel sector. Among the advantages I listed previously, the company's dividend yield and the shareholder payout policy it has demonstrated for several years undoubtedly stand out.

As I mentioned previously, the stable revenue generated by the company thanks to its business model has allowed management to pursue a shareholder payout policy that not only has a dividend yield that far exceeds the average for S&P 500 companies, but also grows.

As can be seen below, the gross dividend payout has increased consistently over the past few years. The amount is expected to continue to increase by 2025, based on the expected FCF the company can generate. This shareholder relationship is, in my view, one of the company's main advantages over other competitors. The other, as we will see later, is the multiples shown at the current share price.

1Q2025 Results

At this point, I'd like to briefly summarize the company's results for the last quarter. Plains reported net income of $443 million in 1Q2025, which represents a speculative increase compared to the $226 million reported in 1Q2024. However, looking at the Adjusted value, we see a 6% year-over-year increase, rising from a total of $354 million in 1Q2024 to $375 million in the latest report.

If we turn to Adjusted EBITDA, we see a similar trend. Year-over-year growth in this case was 4%, going from a total of $847 million in 1Q2024 to $881 million in 1Q2025. If we take this latter value and annualize it, we can compare it to the current EV value of $25.64 billion. In this case, taking this into account, we can see that the EV/EBITDA ratio is 7.27x. While this is somewhat higher compared to other companies I've covered, considering PAA's business model, it is interesting.

I wouldn't want to lose focus on the fact that Plains is a company with a business model that maintains a very stable revenue stream. In that sense, it's to be expected that the multiples are somewhat higher than those displayed by some cyclical companies more closely tied to fossil fuel production. Still, it's interesting to see that it's significantly below many S&P 500 companies, which, when combined with these fairly predictable revenues, is very promising.

Another highlight was net cash from operating activities, which closed the quarter at $639 million. Again, if we annualize this value, we see that the P/CF ratio is 4.62x, which, in my opinion, is attractive for a company with the PAA business model.

One of the things that could be concerning about the company is its debt stock relative to the company's Market Cap. However, as can be seen in the chart above, capital payments are well distributed over time in long-term debt, which, in my opinion, should not create pressure for the company. On the other hand, if we calculate the EBITDA/Interest ratio, we see that it stands at 6.21x, which, again, does not seem like a value that causes concern.

Valuation

At this point, I'd like to look at two models I created to give you an idea of ​​a possible valuation for the company and to gain a broader perspective on the investment thesis I detailed earlier. The first model I wanted to show is a DCF I created, taking into account the details in this report and some assumptions that aim to be as conservative as possible. For the other model, I created a valuation using multiples, using the market values ​​and ratios shown in the SeekingAlpha Peer segment as a reference.

I want to start with the DCF model, which, in my view, seems the most revealing of the company's potential. In that sense, the tax rate I used to perform the calculations was 15%, which, while seemingly low, is clearly above the average when compared to recent years in SeekingAlpha's Financials segment. Regarding the perpetual growth rate, I used a value of 2%, slightly below the average global GDP growth rate and significantly below the growth rate of the fossil fuel industry.

To discount cash flows, we must fully understand two things. First, the large proportion of debt within the capital structure, as previously seen. Second, regarding the cost of equity, it's important to emphasize again that the company is a service company with a fairly stable cash flow. For this reason, a WACC of 10% seems appropriate for the discount. This value, as can be seen, would be significantly higher than the cost of debt, which represents more than 90% of the company's EV. Therefore, I consider a 10% discount on cash flows to be more than conservative given the company's capital structure.

Regarding cash flows, I assumed EBIT growth of 4% in the first year, a growth that decreases over time until it converges to the perpetual growth rate. Regarding depreciation, a decrease over time was also assumed to provide a certain conservative effect to the model's results. Taking all of this into account, it is clear that the company has quite significant upside potential. If we take into account the rather predictable business model, we see that a valuation with these characteristics is more than attractive, in my view.

I didn't add values ​​to the Change in Operating Working Capital because the changes are very small compared to the other values, which are significant and representative for calculating FCF. Therefore, to simplify the model and estimates, I decided not to assign estimated values ​​for future periods and instead set them to zero.

Regarding the valuation multiples, I used companies listed in the SeekingAlpha Peers segment and the ratios presented there as a reference. The only thing worth mentioning is that for the final valuation, I weighted the ratios differently based on those I considered most relevant to explaining the company's metrics. Both the weights and the multiples are shown in the table below. What is interesting is that the upside potential is similar in both models, which in my view lends a certain degree of robustness to the assumptions used in the DCF.

Risks

Among the risks we could list is the sensitivity of the stock price to fluctuations in international oil prices. While it's true that this doesn't affect the company as directly as it might for producers, the markets have reacted negatively with a drop in the share price.

From my perspective, these movements add volatility but do not have a significant impact in the medium and long term, as the company's revenues are, as I mentioned previously, tied to longer-term contracts with production companies. On the other hand, the new tariff regulations that the Trump administration intends to push through have generated marked pessimism in the market.

Once again, these political ups and downs could negatively impact the price in the short term. However, it's important to note that PAA's core business is in the US, and I believe the impact of the new Republican administration's measures won't be as significant.

Conclusion

In general, it can be seen that Plains maintains a business structure that provides relatively stable cash flow in the short and medium term. This has allowed management to pursue an aggressive dividend payment policy that is beneficial to shareholders. Under this premise, Plains has maintained a high and upward dividend yield in recent years, something that is expected to continue to increase as long as FCF generation remains at current levels. Furthermore, with solid demand for fossil fuels and operating in the United States, in the Permian Basin, demand for Plains' infrastructure from the region's major producers is still expected to continue. For all these reasons, combined with a DCF model and a multiple valuation I conducted, I believe the company offers good upside potential for the future.

data.title: HFRO: Controversial Fund, But May Be Too Cheap With A Massive Discount To NAV
data.description:
HFRO Fund History

Highland Opportunities and Income Fund (NYSE:HFRO) is a controversial closed end fund that invests primarily in real estate assets but also holds a smattering of equity, CLOs, fixed and floating rate loans.

It has been about eighteen months since my last article on HFRO.

At the time, I rated HFRO a Hold and recommended a small starter position when it was at a 37% discount and adding more shares if the discount went above 40%. With the benefit of hindsight, it would have been much better to wait until later in 2024 after a surprise 50% distribution cut in January 2024 caused a mass exit from dividend oriented investors.

Overall, the HFRO NAV and market performance has been pretty dismal since late 2023, but there have been some trading opportunities since then to help alleviate the damage. There was a tender offer earlier this year, where you could have exchanged about 46% of your shares for preferred stock.

I decided to write an update now, because I believe the HFRO management may soon be initiating a new share repurchase program. With a 57% discount to NAV, such a program appears to be a no-brainer.

For quite some time now, HFRO has been trading with a discount well above 50% which is the highest discount in the US closed-end fund universe tracked on cefconnect.com.

Why is the Discount So High?

  • Very High Exposure To Illiquid Assets: In the last holdings reports to shareholders as of March 31, 2025. HFRO included the following blurb in a small footnote:

    • “Securities with a total aggregate value of $757,415,160, or 87.4% of net assets, were classified as Level 3 within the three-tier fair value hierarchy”.

    • Note that a significant portion of the Level 3 assets are secured by real estate which is more difficult to value accurately than publicly traded stocks or bonds.

    • Many investors are especially concerned in this case because several of the Level 3 illiquid assets are controlled or related to the NexPoint and Highland management companies. Management benefits from higher management fees if the NAV fair value marks to market are too high.

  • Distribution Cut: The discount widened a lot after a large 50% distribution cut at the end of 2023, and has never really recovered. A higher distribution returns more capital to shareholders allowing them to recover more of the discount. The distribution cut rubbed a lot of income investors the wrong way and they likely dumped their HFRO shares.

  • Highly Complex Portfolio: HFRO holds a widely diverse set of assets which may be hard to understand. Management does do a pretty good job in a quarterly shareholder report of describing the top ten holdings in some detail, but the diversity of assets in both real estate and non-real estate can be difficult to value.

  • Shareholder Concerns About Persistent Discount: The fund management has taken some limited actions to reduce the discount, but has not been aggressive enough in returning capital to shareholders.

    • Two years ago, management initiated a $100 million share buyback program. But only about $20 million of this program was actually utilized, and the buyback program has just ended. I recently sent a message to Shareholder Relations asking if they will be extending the share buyback program going forward.

    • The fund instituted a $100 million tender offer for shareholders this year offering $10 a share. But the $10 a share was not paid in cash and no portfolio assets had to be liquidated. Instead they issued $100 million of a new 5.375% preferred stock (HFRO.PR.B). By doing this, the fund management did not have to return any cash to shareholders, so the management fee which is based on assets under management remained the same. When the new preferred came out, the market price traded around $16, so the effective price that investors could have realized was about $6.40 a share, not $10.00.

  • The “Dondero” Discount: Two closed-end funds run by Highland Capital Management, (NYSE:HFRO) and (HGLB) tend to have high persistent discounts to NAV. There has been a lot of litigation involved in companies controlled by James Dondero. For example Highland Capital Management, an investment advisor controlled by Dondero, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in October, 2019. While this did not directly hurt shareholders in HFRO or HGLB, such events can erode investor confidence.

The recent market performance of HFRO has been mixed, partially because swings in the discount have a much larger effect than the actual NAV performance. More active closed-end fund traders may have benefited from the recent $10 tender offer with a proration of about 46%. It would have been possible to sell off their preferred shares above $16.00 (equivalent to a common share price of $6.40) the first few weeks after the tender offer closed.

Some Recent Developments

1) Since the distribution cut at the end of 2023, the fund has paid out regular monthly distributions of $0.0385. Management has not discussed raising the distribution again. I suspect they would prefer share buybacks which gives them more flexibility in how much is returned to shareholders.

2) The Credit Suisse case, which seems to drag on forever, is back in the Texas fifth circuit court of appeals. The Fund has provided a link for any shareholder who wants to follow the Credit Suisse litigation in detail- TJB | 5th COA The court number is 05-24-01124-CV.

If there are any attorneys reading this article, perhaps they can comment on this case. As a non-attorney, I felt that it is quite possible that HFRO may not receive anything additional, or perhaps only a small amount. Perhaps a compromise will be reached to reduce the legal fees for both sides.

3) The two year $100 million share buyback expired in May 2025. I have sent a note to Investor Relations asking/suggesting that a new share repurchase agreement should be initiated since the discount to NAV is well above 50%.

Some investors would likely feel this is a waste of my time. But I was surprised back in 2023. Here is an excerpt from an old HFRO article I wrote back in November, 2023:

“There were no share repurchases in the second quarter of 2023. I contacted Investor Relations and asked them if they would consider providing more frequent updates of their share repurchase activities. This is done by several other closed-end funds. For example, SPE provides monthly updates of their share buyback activity.

They told me that for now, they only plan to provide updates in the annual and semi-annual shareholder reports. Perhaps if other shareholders request more frequent share buyback updates, the Fund's Board will reconsider this policy.”

I was pleasantly surprised that HFRO did start to report the share repurchase activity in 2024 on a quarterly basis. I would have preferred monthly reporting, similar to SPE, but the quarterly updates are a big improvement over what we had before.

Three Year Discount History for HFRO

Here is the asset allocation breakdown as of March 31, 2025:

Portfolio Allocation Breakdown

Number of Holdings= 72

Leverage Percentage= 26.4%

Equity

18.9%

Real Estate

68.0%

CLOs

3.4%

Loans

9.8%

Source: HFRO Fund Fact Sheet Q1 2025

Real estate is the largest allocation category by far. A more detailed summary of the real estate holdings was presented in the last shareholder presentation. The largest allocations are to Multifamily, Self Storage, Single family Rentals and Life Sciences with little or no allocation to the troubled retail and commercial office sectors.

Portfolio - Top 10 Exposures (as of March 31, 2025)

I know that many investors are highly suspect of the NAV reported by HFRO. But I believe that the NAV reported by HFRO management is probably pretty close to being accurate. A precise valuation of any private investment is always difficult and adds a lot of uncertainty to the NAV.

Keep in mind that the NAV uncertainty can occur in both directions, so it is even possible that The NAV could be understated depending on how things play out in the future with the economy.

The current 57% discount seems more than adequate to account for the added NAV uncertainty, especially if management re-enacts the share repurchase program. Of course this is only true if management actually starts to aggressively buy back shares in the open market.

Trading Opportunities in HFRO

I have owned a core position in HFRO for some time now. Because of big swings in its discount to NAV, there are sometimes opportunities to add shares on a sharp downward move in the price, or to trim shares when the stock bounces up rapidly.

The $100 million tender offer earlier this year also provided some good opportunities, especially for those who aggressively sold the HFRO-B preferred when it first started to trade. I did trim a few of the HFRO-B shares from the tender offer north of $16, but in hindsight I was not nearly aggressive enough. With the preferred trading closer to $15, I am holding my remaining shares and would look to add more shares on dips below $15.

Fund Performance

Here is the year-by-year total return performance record since 2018 when the fund converted to the closed-end fund format. Performance has been poor on both an absolute and relative basis in 2023 and 2024, after a good year in 2022.

Morningstar uses the Bank Loan category for HFRO, but they should probably move HFRO to one of the real estate categories, since the fund has changed considerably over the past few years. The performance numbers do not take into account any gains an investor may have made from the tender offer in 2024.

HFRO NAV Performance

HFRO Market Performance

Bank Loan NAV Performance

Rank in Category (NAV)

2018

+1.53%

-12.15%

- 0.47%

7

2019

+1.48%

+4.20%

+8.82%

94

2020

+4.36%

-8.38%

- 1.26%

1

2021

14.76%

+16.35%

+12.29%

16

2022

6.66%

+ 1.69%

- 7.20%

1

2023

-4.79%

-16.90%

+14.34%

100

2024

-2.36%

-27.20%

YTD

+1.07%

-1.18%

Source: Morningstar

Three Year Annualized Fund Performance

HFRO Price

-17.57%

HFRO NAV

-3.81%

Bank Loan NAV

+7.0%

Source: Morningstar as of June 2, 2025

Alpha is Generated by High Discount + High Distributions

When you invest in HFRO, there is a possibility of capturing some "alpha" from the high discount and distribution yield.

The distribution rate of 9.13% along with the 57.6% discount allows investors to capture some alpha by recovering a small portion of the NAV whenever a monthly distribution is paid out.

When you recover NAV from a fund selling at a 57.6% discount, the percentage gain is 1.00/ 0.424 or about 135.8%. So the annual alpha generated by the 9.13% distribution is computed as:

(0.0913)*(1.358)=0.1239 or about 12.39% a year in discount alpha.

This is way more than the 1.45% baseline expense ratio. As long as the HFRO managers can stabilize performance, the fund should be able to generate an attractive total return going forward.

Future Potential For Activist Activity

Institutions hold about 51.7% of the shares, which is up considerably over the last few years.

Here are some opportunistic investors that owned shares in HFRO as of March 31, 2025:

Activist # of shares

Thomas J. Herzfeld 2,677,303

Matisse Capital 583,387

Bulldog Investors, LLP 10,718

Source: Highland Opportunities and Income Fund Common Shares of Beneficial Interest (HFRO) Holding Activity, Ownership Summary, & Shareholders | Nasdaq

I would love to see Saba Capital join the party. When Boaz Weinstein was asked if he would consider an activist play on HFRO on his Twitter/X feed, he replied "Don't mess with Texas".

Bulldog Investors has reduced its holdings over the last few years, perhaps because of the tender offer. I would love to see them add considerably to their current position. Raymond James is currently the largest institutional investor by far with about 8 million shares. But these holdings most likely aggregate smaller managed accounts from many different financial advisors.

To my knowledge, there is no current activist activity occurring with HFRO. But it is nice to have activist investors on board to keep the fund managers on their toes. There is always the possibility that one or more of the activist owners could start an activist campaign in the future.

Highland Income Fund (HFRO)

  • Total investment exposure: $919 Million

  • Total Common assets: $780 Million

  • Annual Distribution (Market) Yield= 9.13%

  • Last Regular Quarterly Distribution= $0.0385 (Annual= $0.462)

  • Fund Baseline Expense ratio: 1.45%

  • Discount to NAV= -57.62%

  • Effective Leverage: 26%

  • Average Daily Volume (shares)= 234,000 (Source: Yahoo Finance)

  • Average Daily Dollar Trading Volume = $1,180,000

HFRO is a fairly liquid stock and usually trades with a bid-asked spread of one or two cents. But when the market gets slow, the spread can sometimes widen out. There is usually limited size available on both the bid and ask price of less than 1,000 shares. Because of this, I would recommend using multiple smaller orders to accumulate a position. I usually use limit orders, but occasionally use small market orders when the bid-ask spread is only a penny.

I believe that HFRO is at a decent level for a starting purchase or to add to an existing position with the discount above 53%.

data.title: Amex Exploration: Talking About 'Near-Term' Production With A Preliminary Economic Assessment
data.description:
Description

Amex Exploration (TSXV:AMX:CA) is an early-stage mineral exploration company focused on the Perron Property located on Quebec's side of the Abitibi greenstone belt. In recent weeks, Amex completed the strategic acquisition of the Perron West Property, an extensive land package directly adjacent to the Perron Property. As a result, Amex's mining claims now cover 19,752 hectares, which represents a sharp increase versus the 4,560 hectares covered before the transaction.

Under the purchase agreement, Amex will pay the seller an initial cash consideration of $150,000 on top of issuing 1.5 million shares. The seller will retain a 1.5% gross overriding royalty and be eligible for various milestone payments. Given the purchase price and the prime location in the heart of the Abitibi greenstone belt, the acquisition of Perron West makes perfect sense from a strategic point of view.

When I think about the Abitibi greenstone belt, I think about Canadian Malartic's incredible story. During my time in Toronto, in the spring of 2018 to be more precise, I remember having dinner with John Burzynski of Osisko along with other members of Toronto's Young Mining Professionals association. At the time, I was a research analyst at a small hedge fund specializing in natural resources and small-cap investing. I was learning a lot during office hours and having drinks with great people in the evenings. If I look back over my entire career, those were the best times. I also realize that spending time with inspiring people is something that I truly miss now that I have been living in the woods for three years. Of course, there is nothing more awe-inspiring than spending time in Nature, but there is also something uplifting about meeting people passionate about what they do. I feel that many professionals are sleepwalking through their careers without truly being alive. After all, it is just a big game, is it not? Why is there a need to be so serious? Let's play instead!

Anyhow, after producing ~650,000 ounces of gold in 2024, Canadian Malartic is now one of the world's largest gold mines and also one of Agnico's (AEM) crown jewel assets. From a quality point of view, Agnico's portfolio may well be unparalleled in the gold mining industry. A significant proportion of the company's economic value is underpinned by assets located in the Abitibi greenstone belt and in proximity to Amex's land package.

If Canadian Malartic is one of the world's largest gold mines based on production volume, the LaRonde Mine is also quite unique, with proven and probable gold reserves of 2.5 million ounces at an average grade of 6.36 g/t. I plan to research Agnico Eagle in the coming days, but for now, my goal is simply to highlight the prime neighbourhood in which Amex's land package is located.

In January 2024, Eldorado Gold (EGO) announced a CAD$15 million strategic investment in Amex. With a 9.9% interest, Eldorado became Amex's second-largest shareholder, just behind Eric Sprott at ~12%. Eldorado Gold is the first company I have written about after joining this platform in September 2024. The company continues to work diligently on bringing its world-class Skouries Project into production. After numerous delays and budget overruns, commercial production is now expected in mid-2026.

Even though Eldorado's portfolio is currently composed of four operating assets, the impact of Skouries will be substantial and bring free cash flow generation to a new level. The simple fact that Skouries is expected to produce an average of 140,000 ounces of gold at an average cash cost of -$365 over its 20-year mine life is enough to illustrate the point. Eldorado Gold: All Eyes On Skouries

As a reminder, Eldorado also owns the Lamaque Complex in Val d'Or, one of the largest towns within the Abitibi greenstone belt. Throughout 2024, the Lamaque Complex produced almost 200,000 ounces of gold at an average grade of 6.74 g/t. In other words, Eldorado has first-hand experience with high-grade underground mines within the belt. Knowing that it is precisely what Amex is trying to develop at Perron, the relationship between both companies starts to make sense.

Production Talks Based on PEAs

Just a few days ago, Amex published an updated mineral resource estimate on the Perron property. The technical report was based on 46,355 meters of new drilling data in addition to 562,137 meters of historical drill data. After approximately 600,000 meters of drilling, the Perron Property is now estimated to host 1.6 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 6.14 g/t. In terms of grade, Perron is directly comparable to LaRonde and Lamaque, so that's great. However, one of Amex's biggest problems is tonnage, and in my view, it is particularly disappointing after so much drilling and capital spending.

Throughout its life, Amex has raised approximately CAD$140 million in total. So shareholders have injected CAD$140 million, and what did they get in return? The answer is a large land package in a prime location, but with only 1.6 million ounces of M&I resources, which is hardly enough to justify building a mine.

In multiple interviews, Amex's CEO, Victor Cantore, mentioned that the company was moving towards 'near-term production'. However, I was surprised to find out that the company's plans were entirely based upon a preliminary economic assessment, or PEA. As a reminder, producing a PEA is the first step in a project's lifecycle. Generally, it is followed by a pre-feasibility study and finally by a bankable feasibility study. I wonder how the company can talk about near-term production when everything is based on preliminary economic studies. Moreover, after approximately 600,000 meters of drilling and after raising ~CAD$140 million, I am quite surprised that there is no pre-feasibility study, to say the least.

I am the first to admit that Perron's economics look great according to the most recent PEA published in November 2024. We are talking about an after-tax IRR of ~40% and a quick payback period of less than two years. It looks great, but how much is it worth knowing that it is all based on preliminary modelling?

How many remember the story of Rubicon Minerals and the Phoenix Deposit in the Red Lake camp of Ontario? Similar to Amex, the results of Rubicon's PEA were fantastic, and the company went straight to production without any pre-feasibility or feasibility studies. The results were disastrous, and Rubicon eventually filed for bankruptcy. It is only after the first gold pour was expected that the company realized Phoenix's geology was more complex than previously thought. Ironically, this is exactly the kind of information that you find out when doing pre-feasibility and feasibility studies. I am not saying that Amex is the next Rubicon Minerals, but Perron's gold mineralization does not look so simple to extract, with multiple open pits and underground workings required.

Rubicon did look like a near-perfect story: A high-grade discovery in Northern Ontario’s legendary Red Lake camp, home of the world’s richest gold mine. But this company had numerous skeptics, and they shared a concern about its strategy. Put simply, Rubicon tried to rush a challenging project into production,and it skipped important steps along the way. It went right from the PEA to construction, raising hundreds of millions along the way. What went wrong at Rubicon Minerals

Instead of talking about short-term production, why are we not talking about a pre-feasibility study in the first place? Why the rush to enter into production? Is it just a marketing technique to stir up excitement among retail investors, or do they really plan to move ahead? I don't know the answer, but I think it is worth asking. After all, it is my sole responsibility to protect my hard-earned capital. If I make a poor investment decision and lose a portion of my capital, I have nobody else to blame except myself. I could try blaming the wild creatures surrounding me, but I doubt it would lead to anything constructive.

Capital Spending

According to Amex's 2024 financial statements, travel and representation expenses totalled CAD$0.5 million throughout 2024, on top of another ~CAD$0.5 million in advertising and congress fees. It may not seem like much, but the money spent on travel and conferences does not go in the ground. When looking at Amex's travel calendar for 2025, management will visit the Bahamas, South Africa, Hong Kong, Switzerland, Sweden and the United Arab Emirates, among many other destinations. In my view, it is definitely important to maintain strong relations with the investor community, but we are talking about a mining exploration company that is still generating zero revenues.

Once consulting fees, salaries and bonuses are included, the burn rate increases to almost CAD$3.0 million per year. Based on the current market capitalization of ~CAD$180 million, it is equivalent to a 'time decay' of almost 2.0% per year. Once the capital required to fund the various drilling programs is included, it becomes clear why Amex's number of shares outstanding has increased rapidly over the last few years. As illustrated below, the number of shares outstanding has basically doubled since 2020. How many more shares will have to be issued before reaching first production, if Perron ever reaches that point?

Conclusion

Amex owns a valuable land package totalling 19,752 hectares in the heart of the Abitibi greenstone belt. In addition, approximately 600,000 meters of drilling have been completed on the Perron Property. After raising ~CAD$140 million over the years, Amex recently announced a mineral resource estimate containing 1.6 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 6.14 g/t. There is no doubt that some tangible value must be attributed to that. In my view, this asset base may be particularly attractive for an intermediate producer like Eldorado, for example. With rising gold prices, an increasing amount of capital could trickle down into mineral exploration.

My key issue with Amex is the concept that management talks about near-term production when the Perron Property is still in the preliminary economic assessment phase. I do not want to draw direct parallels, but it strangely reminds me of Rubicon's story with the Phoenix Deposit.

In terms of capital allocation, I also question the concept of attending so many conferences all around the world. Before investing in a junior mineral exploration company without any revenues, I would prefer the tightest cost structure realistically possible. Assuming a constant amount of drilling, the tightest cost structure translates into less dilution over time for the shareholders. Of course, the financial interests of the management team may not necessarily coincide with my own. This is the definition of 'Agency Risk', and I think it is a concept worth keeping in mind, not just for Amex, but for any publicly listed entity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
